question: The blackboard frame for the 2D version shows a grid with labels for rows and columns and defines a 2D peak condition. What does the professor say is the worst-case complexity of the greedy ascent algorithm for this problem, and what does it reduce to when the matrix dimensions are equal?
answer: The blackboard shows the 2D version with an n-row by m-column matrix and defines a 2D peak using conditions a ≥ b, a ≥ c, a ≥ d, a ≥ e. The professor states that a greedy ascent algorithm for the 2D peak-finding problem has Θ(nm) complexity in the worst case, because one may end up touching a large fraction of all elements in the matrix. When N equals M, this reduces to Θ(n²) complexity, which is significantly worse.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard showing 2D version grid with n rows and m columns and 2D peak definition
Professor states Θ(nm) and Θ(n²) when m=n for greedy ascent algorithm

question: What text appears on the blackboard when the professor first introduces the specific problem that will be the focus of the lecture?
answer: When the professor introduces the main problem for the lecture, the blackboard displays the text 'Peak finder' written in white chalk, with 'Peak' in a larger font size than 'finder.' question: How does the professor define the 21st-century notion of 'large' input size at the beginning of the lecture, and how does he contrast it with what was considered large in previous decades?
answer: The professor states that the 21st-century definition of 'large' is roughly a trillion. He contrasts this by humorously noting that when he was a student, 'large' meant about a thousand, and when Professor Erik Demaine was a student, 'large' meant about a million. He further speculates that by the time current students are professors teaching the class, a trillion will seem small and they will be concerned with inputs on the order of 10 to the 18.

ground_truth_spans.description: Professor says 21st century definition of large is a trillion and contrasts with thousand and million
Professor speculates future students will deal with 10^18 as common input size

question: What specific RSA-related example does the professor give when introducing the numerics module, and what connection does he make to a widely used internet protocol?
answer: The professor explains that RSA encryption is used in SSL, and specifically when users access HTTPS websites, RSA is running at the back end. In RSA, prime numbers with thousands of bits in their representation are used, which means 32 bits or even 64 bits are insufficient. This motivates the numerics module, which covers how to handle so-called 'infinite precision numbers' — numbers with thousands of bits — and how to write algorithms in Python that can deal with such large number representations.

ground_truth_spans.description: Professor describes RSA encryption used in SSL/HTTPS with prime numbers thousands of bits long

question: What does the blackboard show as the final complexity result for the divide-and-conquer 1D peak finder, and what specific running time in seconds did the instructor report for the Theta(n) algorithm on an input of 10 million elements?
answer: The blackboard shows the final complexity of the divide-and-conquer 1D peak finder as Theta(log base 2 of n). The instructor reported that the Theta(n) algorithm takes 13 seconds when run on an input of approximately 10 million elements.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows blackboard with T(n)=T(n/2)+Theta(1), base case T(1)=Theta(1), and the result Theta(log2 n times)
Instructor states that on n=10 million, the Theta(n) algorithm takes 13 seconds and the divide-and-conquer takes 0.001 seconds

question: What does the frame caption reveal is written on the blackboard regarding the 'Course Overview' section, and how does the instructor's description of the human genome relate to the concept shown on that board?
answer: The frame caption reveals that the blackboard lists 'Efficient procedures for solving large-scale problems' and 'Scalability' under Course Overview. The instructor describes the human genome as having a billion letters as an example of a large input, directly illustrating why scalability and efficient procedures for large-scale problems are the focus of the course.

question: What does the frame caption show is written on the blackboard about the 'Attempt #2' algorithm for 2D peak finding, and what does the instructor say happens when the condition at position (i,j) is satisfied without picking left or right?
answer: The frame caption shows the blackboard lists: pick middle column j=m/2, find global max on column j at (i,j), compare (i,j-1), (i,j), (i,j+1), and pick left columns if (i,j-1) > (i,j). The instructor states that if neither the left nor the right condition fires, meaning (i,j) is greater than or equal to both (i,j-1) and (i,j+1), then (i,j) is a 2D peak and the algorithm is done.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows Attempt #2 steps on blackboard including pick middle column, find global max, compare neighbors
Instructor explains that if (i,j) >= (i,j-1) and (i,j+1) then (i,j) is a 2D peak because it was the column maximum

question: Why does the instructor say the first attempt at a 2D divide-and-conquer peak finder is incorrect, and what specific numerical example is given to illustrate the failure?
answer: The instructor says the algorithm is incorrect because a 2D peak may not exist on the row selected by the algorithm. The specific example given is a matrix where, starting from a middle row, the algorithm finds 14 as a 1D peak on that row, but 14 is not a 2D peak because larger values such as 19 exist in adjacent positions in the matrix.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor states algorithm is incorrect because a 2D peak may not exist on row i
Instructor gives example: algorithm returns 14 as 1D peak on the chosen row but 14 is not a 2D peak
